## NIST signature on-ramp round 3 — algorithm inclusion strategy

NIST recently announced the results of round 3 of the signature on-ramp process, shortlisting 9 of the original 14 candidates. Rodrigo proposed that including all remaining on-ramp algorithms in liboqs should be a **top priority for the 0.17 release** (not 0.16, which is already in progress). Of the 9 shortlisted algorithms, liboqs currently includes 4; 5 remain to be integrated.

Discussion covered the following points:

**Outreach to submission teams.** Rodrigo proposed reaching out to each remaining submission team as a first step. Teams are currently focused on preparing round 3 resubmissions (deadline: August 14, 2026), so active collaboration is not expected until autumn. The HQC team was cited as an example: when contacted, their first question was about integration effort. Michael suggested drawing on the MQOM integration experience and possibly inviting the MQOM contributor (Ryan) to give a brief presentation at a status meeting, to help other teams calibrate the effort. Basil agreed this would be useful.

**Preparation during summer.** While submission teams are occupied with their resubmissions, Rodrigo proposed using the summer to update integration documentation and improve scripting and tooling for algorithm inclusion. Douglas and Bruce agreed to discuss the current state of that scripting. Norm suggested producing a recorded demo of the scripting workflow. Michael raised the possibility of using AI tooling to help automate or accelerate parts of the integration.

**Mentorships.** Douglas and Rodrigo agreed the PQCA mentorship program is a key resource, both in the traditional mentor/mentee model and in a variant where mentor and mentee both come from a submission team. Douglas noted this requires mentors from within the OQS team to volunteer.

**Prioritization.** Rodrigo does not want to formally prioritize among the 5 remaining algorithms; the intent is to target all of them for 0.17, with first-come-first-served as the tie-breaker if capacity is constrained.

**Upstream motivation.** Basil noted that integrating with liboqs while preparing a round 3 submission benefits submission teams by providing additional build and test coverage. He cited the skSign project (in which he is involved) as planning to prepare liboqs integration in parallel with the round 3 submission. He also flagged that some integrations may require changes on the liboqs side (e.g., handling large numbers of included files), which is an additional argument for mentorship support.

Important

**Action (Rodrigo):** Open tracking issues in liboqs for each of the 5 remaining on-ramp algorithms and add them to the 0.17 milestone.

Important

**Action (Rodrigo):** Reach out to the 5 remaining submission teams to gauge interest in collaborating on liboqs integration.

Important

**Action (Douglas / Bruce):** Discuss and document the current state of scripting/tooling for algorithm inclusion, with a view to improving onboarding material over the summer.

Important

**Action (all):** Consider proposing or mentoring a PQCA mentorship project focused on algorithm integration ([issue #240](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/240)).

## Security response process

Douglas reported that the number of incoming security reports has increased noticeably in recent months, in part due to AI-assisted vulnerability scanning tools being used by external contributors. The existing security response process (written by Spencer last summer) has not scaled well in practice.

**Trail of Bits engagement.** Douglas has accepted an offer from Trail of Bits, sponsored by OpenAI to support open source projects. They will dedicate one engineer to OQS for a week in late June to identify, triage, and submit patches for bugs using AI-assisted tooling. Bruce will be the primary OQS contact. Douglas intends to use the engagement to also gather input on improving the security response process. Loganaden noted from experience with other open source projects that AI-generated reports carry a significant false-positive rate and still require human expertise to review.

**Intake pathways.** Michael proposed consolidating security reports on GitHub — either as open issues or security advisories — and reducing reliance on the `security@openquantumsafe.org` email alias. Douglas agreed in principle, suggesting GitHub be the preferred intake channel while keeping the email alias as a fallback for those who cannot use GitHub. Rodrigo noted that GitHub security advisories already support private disclosure and are appropriate for reports that should not be public before a fix is ready. Norm echoed the importance of a pre-disclosure review period. Douglas will look into using a private GitHub project dashboard to improve the security team's overview of open reports.

**Project Glasswing / Anthropic.** Loganaden mentioned that Anthropic's Project Glasswing (access to Claude Mythos Preview for open source security scanning) allows project teams to request a scan through a named contact. Douglas noted that Hart had been exploring LF-level access; Rodrigo agreed the next step is to follow up with Hart before pursuing this independently.

Important

**Action (Michael):** Open a TSC issue to discuss consolidating security intake to GitHub and deprioritising the email alias, incorporating Douglas's suggestion to retain it as a fallback.

Important

**Action (Douglas):** Explore using a private GitHub project dashboard for internal tracking of security reports.

Important

**Action (Rodrigo / Douglas):** Follow up with Hart on the status of LF access to Anthropic's Project Glasswing / Mythos Preview security scanning.

## Google.org funding discussion

Douglas reported on a call held in May with the google.org team (Google's philanthropic arm), facilitated by Hart. Google.org expressed interest in supporting open source projects working on the post-quantum transition, with particular focus on small and medium enterprises. They identified OQS as a relevant driver of that transition and asked for a proposal.

The most likely funding model is a university-hosted grant, as the Linux Foundation does not have the infrastructure for directly funding project staffing of this kind. The goal would be to bring on a second person at the University of Waterloo (alongside Bruce) to work on OQS. Douglas plans to submit an initial proposal next week and expects an iterative back-and-forth to clarify scope.

Rodrigo's impression from the meeting was that Google.org is open to letting OQS define how the support is used; the exact scope will become clearer through the proposal process. Michael cautioned that such offers rarely come without conditions and encouraged the team to clarify expectations carefully.

Important

**Action (Douglas):** Submit the initial funding proposal to Google.org next week and keep the TSC informed of progress.

## TSC vice chair role starting September 2026

Douglas recapped the current structure: Rodrigo and Douglas are co-chairing during a transition period, with Rodrigo taking over as sole chair in September. The TSC charter calls for a vice chair.

Rodrigo described what he envisions for the role: (a) covering for the chair when unavailable, and (b) acting as a sounding board for agenda items and priorities before meetings. He emphasised this is not a co-chair arrangement — he intends to carry all committed responsibilities himself.

Norman suggested the vice chair should ideally be someone with long-standing experience in the project, to provide continuity and institutional knowledge. Rodrigo acknowledged his own relative newness and agreed this would be valuable. Douglas noted he had not made good use of his own vice chair in the past.

No candidate came forward during the meeting. Rodrigo will open a GitHub issue to continue the discussion.

Important

**Action (Rodrigo):** Open a GitHub issue to solicit candidates and continue discussion on the TSC vice chair role.

## OQS presentations at appropriate conferences

Rodrigo reported that draft proposals have been submitted for three conferences:

- **OpenSSL Conference** (October 13–15, Prague) — submission deadline May 31, 2026.
- **Open Source Summit** (Linux Foundation event, Prague, the week before the OpenSSL Conference).
- **PKI Consortium Conference** (December 1–3, Amsterdam).

The proposals share a common message: OQS remains active and relevant beyond the initial PQC standardization wave, with its focus on research algorithms, new primitives, and hybrid constructions continuing to be important. Responses are not expected until late July or August.

Michael noted that the original intention was to align on the conference message as a group before submitting proposals, and asked whether the submitted abstracts reflected the discussion from the TSC issues. Rodrigo acknowledged this and committed to updating the relevant issues with the submitted text so the team can comment and, if necessary, refine the message for future submissions.

Rodrigo also raised the question of funding for travel and attendance beyond the presenter alone, and plans to discuss with Hart what the process is for requesting PQCA support for this (direct request, or via Norm as TAC representative).

Important

**Action (Rodrigo):** Update the conference messaging issues with the submitted proposal text for team review and comment.

Important

**Action (Rodrigo):** Discuss with Hart the process for requesting PQCA travel funding for conference attendance.
